clean_story:
Stretched seconds. Shrunken hours.
Time perception science studies why clocks tick steady, but your mind doesn’t.

The effect is simplest to spot when you’re bored: a 60-second wait feels like five minutes. But during a car crash, those same five minutes might compress into a single, slow-motion second. Your brain isn’t a stopwatch—it’s a storyteller, stretching or shrinking time based on attention and memory.

Here’s how it works: When scared or excited, your brain’s locus coeruleus floods your system with norepinephrine, a chemical that sharpens focus. More details get encoded per second, so later, when you recall the event, it feels longer—like a video played in slow motion. The opposite happens when distracted. If you zone out during class, your brain records fewer "frames," making the hour vanish like a skipped song.

Tests prove this. In 2007, volunteers free-falling from a 50-meter tower estimated their descent took 36% longer than it did. Yet in lab experiments where people counted tones, boring tasks made time drag, while complex ones (like solving puzzles) made it fly. Even body temperature twists perception: a 2014 study showed people with higher fevers judged intervals as shorter, as if their inner clock ran faster.

Three related subjects are body clocks circadian, dream time distortion, and how animals sense time.
